perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York applicable to agreements made and to be performed in that state, without regard to any of its principles of conflicts of laws or other laws that would result in the application of the laws of another jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted without regard to any presumption against the party causing this Agreement to be drafted. Each of the parties hereby unconditionally and irrevocably waives the right to a trial by jury in any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby. Each of the parties unconditionally and irrevocably consents to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the State of New York located in the County of New York and the Federal district court for the Southern District of New York located in the County of New York with respect to any suit, action or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby, and each of the parties hereby unconditionally and irrevocably waives any objection to venue in any such court.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York applicable to agreements made and to be performed in that state, without regard to any of its principles of conflicts of laws or other laws that would result in the application of the laws of another jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted without regard to any presumption against the party causing this Agreement to be drafted.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the jury trial waiver clause removes consumer protection, which is illegal, especially when there is an imbalance of power. A blanket waiver is looked at with extreme scrutiny.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1, Section 2 of the New York Constitution guarantees the right to a jury trial. While this right can be waived, such waivers must be knowing, voluntary, and intelligent. The changed text removes the explicit waiver, but depending on the circumstances, a court might find that the agreement's silence on the issue coupled with unequal bargaining power could invalidate any implicit waiver, especially regarding complex transactions or if the waiving party did not fully understand the implications. Furthermore, standard boilerplate waivers are highly scrutinized and can be deemed unlawful, particularly if they are buried within lengthy contracts and not explicitly brought to the attention of the waiving party.

Article I, New York Constitution From Ballotpedia Jump to: navigation , search New York Constitution Preamble Articles I • II • III • IV • V • VI • VII • VIII • IX • X • XI • XII • XIII • XIV • XV • XVI • XVII • XVIII • XIX • XX Contents 1 Section 1 1.1 Amendments 2 Section 2 2.1 Amendments 3 Section 3 3.1 Amendments 4 Section 4 4.1 Amendments 5 Section 5 6 Section 6 6.1 Amendments 7 Section 7 7.1 Amendments 8 Section 8 8.1 Amendments 9 Section 9 9.1 Amendments 10 Section 10 11 Section 11 11.1 Amendments 12 Section 12 12.1 Amendments 13 Section 13 14 Section 14 14.1 Amendments 15 Section 15 16 Section 16 16.1 Amendments 17 Section 17 17.1 Amendments 18 Section 18 18.1 Amendments 19 Section 19 19.1 Amendments 20 See also 21 External links 22 Additional reading 23 Footnotes Article I of the New York Constitution is entitled Bill of Rights and consists of 18 sections, three of which have been repealed. Section 1 Text of Section 1: Rights, Privileges and Franchise Secured; Power of Legislature to Dispense with Primary Elections in Certain Cases No member of this state shall be disfranchised, or deprived of any of the rights or privileges secured to any citizen thereof, unless by the law of the land, or the judgment of his or her peers, except that the legislature may provide that there shall be no primary election held to nominate candidates for public office or to elect persons to party positions for any political party or parties in any unit of representation of the state from which such candidates or persons are nominated or elected whenever there is no contest or contests for such nominations or election as may be prescribed by general law. [1] Amendments Amended by vote of the people on November 3, 1959. Amended by vote of the people on November 6, 2001. Section 2 Text of Section 2: Trial by Jury; How Waived Trial by jury in all cases in which it has heretofore been guaranteed by constitutional provision shall remain inviolate forever; but a jury trial may be waived by the parties in all civil cases in the manner to be prescribed by law. The legislature may provide, however, by law, that a verdict may be rendered by not less than five-sixths of the jury in any civil case. A jury trial may be waived
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the clause where all parties waive their right to a trial by jury, which directly contradicts the consumer protection by removing an explicit waiver of a constitutional right. The Perturbation Explanation states that removing the jury trial waiver clause removes consumer protection and that blanket waivers are looked at with extreme scrutiny. The Law Explanation also states that while this right can be waived, such waivers must be knowing, voluntary, and intelligent. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Article I, Section 2 of the New York Constitution states: 'Trial by jury in all cases in which it has heretofore been guaranteed by constitutional provision shall remain inviolate forever; but a jury trial may be waived by the parties in all civil cases in the manner to be prescribed by law.' By omitting the waiver, the altered contract removes a legally significant protection, potentially rendering any implicit waiver invalid, particularly when there's unequal bargaining power. This aligns with the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction' where a legally mandated consumer protection is omitted.

perturbation.original_text: Seller understands and agrees that the business of NIM and its Affiliates is based upon specialized work and Confidential Information (as hereinafter defined). Seller agrees that following the termination of Seller's employment or consulting period with NIM or any Affiliate of NIM and for all times thereafter, he shall keep secret all such Confidential Information and that he will not, directly or indirectly, use for his own benefit or for the benefit of others nor Disclose (as hereinafter defined), without the prior written consent of NIM, any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller understands and agrees that the business of NIM and its Affiliates is based upon specialized work and Confidential Information (as hereinafter defined). Seller agrees that following the termination of Seller's employment or consulting period with NIM or any Affiliate of NIM he shall keep secret all such Confidential Information and that he will not, directly or indirectly, use for his own benefit or for the benefit of others nor Disclose (as hereinafter defined), without the prior written consent of NIM, any Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'and for all times thereafter', there is now no explicit confidentiality after the Seller's termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: The omission of 'and for all times thereafter' weakens the confidentiality obligations. Under New York Trade Secrets Law (N.Y. C.P.L.R. § 4548), trade secrets are protected even after employment terminates. The changed text implies confidentiality only during the employment/consulting period. This potentially contradicts the law, which protects against misappropriation of trade secrets beyond the term of employment, especially if the information continues to qualify as a trade secret.

Privileged communications; electronic communication thereof. No communication privileged under this article shall lose its privileged character for the sole reason that it is communicated by electronic means or because persons necessary for the delivery or facilitation of such electronic communication may have access to the content of the communication.
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures confidentiality 'for all times thereafter', which aligns with the intention of protecting trade secrets even after termination of employment, as indicated in the Law Explanation. The changed text removes this explicit perpetual obligation, implying that confidentiality ends upon termination. Although the scraped snippets are not directly relevant, the Law Explanation, in conjunction with the removal of 'and for all times thereafter' in the changed text, makes the contract contradict the spirit of trade secret protection. The altered contract weakens trade secret protections.

perturbation.original_text: If any action for breach of or to enforce the provisions of this Agreement is commenced, the court in such action shall award to the party in whose favor a judgment is entered, a reasonable sum as attorneys' fees and costs. Such attorneys' fees and costs shall be paid by the non-prevailing party in such action.
perturbation.changed_text: If any action for breach of or to enforce the provisions of this Agreement is commenced, the court in such action may award attorneys' fees and costs.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall award' to 'may award', the clause is now discretionary, and therefore gives less of a guarantee of costs being covered, which is a consumer protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause mandates the awarding of attorneys' fees to the prevailing party. The modified version makes it discretionary ('may award'). This omission potentially contradicts N.Y. Real Prop. Law § 234, which concerns reciprocal attorneys' fees in leases. While this agreement isn't explicitly a lease, the principle of ensuring a level playing field regarding legal costs, especially when one party has significantly more resources, is a consumer protection principle. By making fee awards discretionary, the modified clause disadvantages the party with fewer resources, discouraging them from pursuing legitimate claims.

Right to recover attorneys' fees in actions or summary proceedings arising out of leases of residential property. 1. Whenever a lease of residential property shall provide that in any action or summary proceeding the landlord may recover attorneys' fees and/or expenses incurred as the result of the failure of the tenant to perform any covenant or agreement contained in such lease, or that amounts paid by the landlord therefor shall be paid by the tenant as additional rent, there shall be implied in such lease a covenant by the landlord to pay to the tenant the reasonable attorneys' fees and/or expenses i
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates attorneys' fees to the prevailing party, while the modified text makes it discretionary. The Law Explanation points to N.Y. Real Prop. Law § 234, which concerns reciprocal attorneys' fees in leases. The provided snippet from NYSenate.gov regarding SECTION 234, 'Right to recover attorneys' fees in actions or summary proceedings arising out of leases of residential property,' supports the idea of a legal basis for mandatory fee recovery in certain contexts. While the contract may not explicitly be a lease, removing the mandatory aspect ('shall award' to 'may award') undermines the principle of ensuring a level playing field regarding legal costs, potentially disadvantaging the party with fewer resources, as explained in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the restrictive covenants contained in Sections 1, 2 and/or 3 of this Agreement shall be found by a court of competent jurisdiction to be unreasonable by reason of such restrictive covenants extending for too great a period of time or over too great a geographic area or by reason of such restrictive covenants being too extensive in any other respect, then such restrictive covenant shall be deemed modified to the minimum extent necessary to make such restrictive covenant reasonable and enforceable under the circumstances.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the restrictive covenants contained in Sections 1, 2 and/or 3 of this Agreement shall be found by a court of competent jurisdiction to be unreasonable by reason of such restrictive covenants extending for too great a period of time or over too great a geographic area or by reason of such restrictive covenants being too extensive in any other respect, then such restrictive covenant shall remain as is.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the 'blue pencil doctrine', which allowed for modification of the document to be reasonable. Now it just remains.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York's "Reasonableness" Standard for Restrictive Covenants
perturbation.law_citation: Reed, Roberts Assocs., Inc. v. Strauman, 40 N.Y.2d 303, 307 (1976)
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the 'blue pencil' provision, the contract no longer attempts to conform to New York's reasonableness standard for restrictive covenants. New York courts generally enforce restrictive covenants only to the extent that they are reasonable in time, geographic area, and scope. The modified text implies that an unreasonable covenant will stand, contradicting this established legal principle. This creates a risk that an overbroad covenant would be unenforceable, potentially harming the party seeking to enforce it, or unfairly burdening the other party.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the 'blue pencil doctrine,' which previously allowed a court to modify unreasonable restrictive covenants to make them enforceable. The altered text states the unreasonable covenant will remain 'as is,' directly contradicting the principle that covenants should be reasonable to be enforceable. The Law Explanation supports this by stating New York courts enforce restrictive covenants only to the extent they are reasonable in time, geographic area, and scope. Without the 'blue pencil' provision, an unreasonable covenant would stand, conflicting with established legal principles. Therefore, the modification leads to a clear non-compliance, as it attempts to enforce something potentially unenforceable.
